# Verify `sesam user remove-recipient` drops a recipient from a user and
# rotates the audit key so the removed key loses access, while a surviving
# recipient keeps it. Removing the last recipient is refused.

exec git init
exec git config user.email test@sesam.dev
exec git config user.name 'Sesam Test'

exec sesam init --user admin
exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'init sesam'

# Add bob with two recipients: his own key and carol's (a second device).
exec sesam tell --user bob --recipient $BOB_PUBKEY --recipient $CAROL_PUBKEY --group dev

# Both keys can decrypt the audit log to start with.
exec sesam --identity $BOB_KEY show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl
exec sesam --identity $CAROL_KEY show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl

# Remove carol's key from bob.
exec sesam user remove-recipient --user bob --recipient $CAROL_PUBKEY

# The audit log records the operation...
exec sesam show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl
stdout '"user.rm_recipients"'
stdout '"bob"'

# ...and `sesam log` renders it with the remove glyph and description.
exec sesam log
stdout 'removed recipient of bob'

# The removed key is locked out (RotateKey), the surviving key still works.
! exec sesam --identity $CAROL_KEY show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl
exec sesam --identity $BOB_KEY show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl

exec sesam verify

# The last remaining recipient cannot be removed.
! exec sesam user remove-recipient --user bob --recipient $BOB_PUBKEY
